revision.text:
[[de:Horusgeleit]] '''Horus Procession''' is an ancient Egyptian festival ==Overview== {{Infobox hieroglyphs two-columns |NAME = T18-S29-G43-D54:Z2-G5
Schemsu-{{ill|Horus|en|Horus|qid=Q84122|lt=Hor|1=Horus}}
''Šmsw-Ḥr.w''
''Servant of Horus / Horus Retinue / Horus Following'' |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Altes Reich (Ägypten)|de|Altes Reich (Ägypten)|ja|Altes Reich (Ägypten)|zh|Altes Reich (Ägypten)|lt=Old Kingdom (Egypt)|lt_ja=Old Kingdom (Egypt)|lt_de=Old Kingdom (Egypt)|lt_zh=Old Kingdom (Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Altes Reich (Ägypten)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |NAME2-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Military colours, standards and guidons|en|Military colours, standards and guidons|qid=Q2912707|lt=Fourfold Standard|1=Military colours, standards and guidons}} of the king The original hieroglyph of the standard of Chons (Imiut fetish) cannot currently be displayed in Wgoodbye $1’s font set. |NAME2 = E18-G7-G26-R12-Aa1:N35:M23-G43
{{ill|Wepwawet|en|Wepwawet|qid=Q372560|lt=Upuaut|1=Wepwawet}}, {{ill|Horus|en|Horus|qid=Q84122|lt=Horus|1=Horus}}, {{ill|Thoth (disambiguation)|en|Thoth (disambiguation)|qid=Q234267|lt=Thoth|1=Thoth (disambiguation)}} and {{ill|Khonsu|en|Khonsu|qid=Q190521|lt=Chons|1=Khonsu}} |NAME3-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Military colours, standards and guidons|en|Military colours, standards and guidons|qid=Q2912707|lt=Open Standard|1=Military colours, standards and guidons}} of the king |NAME3 = R12-E20-E15-G6-R23
{{ill|Seth (Egyptian mythology)|de|Seth (Egyptian mythology)|ja|Seth (Egyptian mythology)|zh|Seth (Egyptian mythology)|lt=Seth|lt_ja=Seth|lt_de=Seth|lt_zh=Seth|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seth (Egyptian mythology)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Anubis|en|Anubis|qid=Q47534|lt=Anubis|1=Anubis}} and {{ill|Min (Egyptian mythology)|de|Min (Egyptian mythology)|ja|Min (Egyptian mythology)|zh|Min (Egyptian mythology)|lt=Min|lt_ja=Min|lt_de=Min|lt_zh=Min|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Min (Egyptian mythology)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |BILD1 = NarmerPalette-CloseUpOfProcession-ROM.png |BILD1-BREITE = 300px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Horus retinue on the Narmer Palette }} The '''Horus retinue''' (also '''Servants of Horus, Festival of the Journey Through the Land''') {{ill|symbol|de|symbol|ja|symbol|zh|symbol|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/symbol|1=Symbol (typeface)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q187829}} initially symbolized the {{ill|Pharaoh|de|Pharaoh|ja|Pharaoh|zh|Pharaoh|lt=King|lt_ja=King|lt_de=King|lt_zh=King|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pharaoh|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as a living {{ill|Horus|en|Horus|qid=Q84122|lt=Horus|1=Horus}}, in whose train other deities of {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|qid=Q11768|lt=Ancient Egypt|1=Ancient Egypt}} stood, and is attested in {{ill|Egyptian Mythology|de|Egyptian Mythology|ja|Egyptian Mythology|zh|Egyptian Mythology|lt=Ancient Egyptian mythology|lt_ja=Ancient Egyptian mythology|lt_de=Ancient Egyptian mythology|lt_zh=Ancient Egyptian mythology|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptian Mythology|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as early as the {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|de|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|ja|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|zh|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|lt=Thinite Period|lt_ja=Thinite Period|lt_de=Thinite Period|lt_zh=Thinite Period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. At that time, Horus was regarded as the all-encompassing sky god and shows connections to the goddess {{ill|Mafdet|en|Mafdet|qid=Q845626|lt=Mafdet|1=Mafdet}}, who, as the bearer of the {{ill|Leopard skin (clothing in Ancient Egypt)|en|Leopard skin (clothing in Ancient Egypt)|qid=Q1486568|lt=Sky Leopard Skin|1=Leopard skin (clothing in Ancient Egypt)}}, possessed characteristics later attributed to {{ill|Osiris|en|Osiris|qid=Q46491|lt=Osiris|1=Osiris}}. Mit the introduction in the {{ill|3rd Dynasty|de|3rd Dynasty|ja|3rd Dynasty|zh|3rd Dynasty|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/3rd Dynasty|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the worship of the {{ill|Sun God|de|Sun God|ja|Sun God|zh|Sun God|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun God|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{ill|Re (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (Egyptian mythology)|ja|Re (Egyptian mythology)|zh|Re (Egyptian mythology)|lt=Re|lt_ja=Re|lt_de=Re|lt_zh=Re|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Re (Egyptian mythology)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a change in significance ensued, as the king now represented the personified sun deity as the firstborn son of Re. With the later establishment of {{ill|Court (disambiguation)|en|Court (disambiguation)|qid=Q293634|lt=Court|1=Court (disambiguation)}} halls in {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Egypt)|ja|Memphis (Egypt)|zh|Memphis (Egypt)|lt=Memphis|lt_ja=Memphis|lt_de=Memphis|lt_zh=Memphis|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Memphis (Egypt)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the processions of the Horus retinue came to an end – along with the associated {{ill|Cattle count|de|Cattle Count in Ancient Egypt|ja|Cattle Count in Ancient Egypt|zh|Cattle Count in Ancient Egypt|lt=Cattle Count|lt_ja=Cattle Count|lt_de=Cattle Count|lt_zh=Cattle Count|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cattle count|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Judiciary|de|Judiciary|ja|Judiciary|zh|Judiciary|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Judiciary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. == Royal Cult == In the depictions on the {{ill|Turin Royal Papyrus|de|Turin Royal Papyrus|ja|Turin Royal Papyrus|zh|Turin Royal Papyrus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Turin Royal Papyrus|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the early kings are also listed, explicitly designated with respect to their {{ill|Horus name|de|Horus Names|ja|Horus Names|zh|Horus Names|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horus name|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} as “Servants of Horus” or “Successors of Horus”. The calendrical entries on the {{ill|Palermo Stone|de|Palermo Stone|ja|Palermo Stone|zh|Palermo Stone|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Palermo Stone|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} title the cattle count, in the context of tax levies, as the solemn {{ill|Procession|de|Procession|ja|Procession|zh|Procession|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Procession|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the “Horus retinue”. === Fourfold Standard === On the {{ill|Narmer Palette|de|Narmer Palette|ja|Narmer Palette|zh|Narmer Palette|lt=Narmer Palette|lt_ja=Narmer Palette|lt_de=Narmer Palette|lt_zh=Narmer Palette|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Narmer Palette|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the king, during a procession, wears the {{ill|Lower Egypt|de|Lower Egypt|ja|Lower Egypt|zh|Lower Egypt|lt=red crown of Lower Egypt|lt_ja=red crown of Lower Egypt|lt_de=red crown of Lower Egypt|lt_zh=red crown of Lower Egypt|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lower Egypt|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, followed by a sandal-bearer whose title is marked with the {{ill|Ideogram|de|Ideogram|ja|Ideogram|zh|Ideogram|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ideogram|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the goddess {{ill|Seshat|en|Seshat|qid=Q478490|lt=Seschat|1=Seshat}}. Preceding the king is a panther-pelt bearer designated as {{ill|Tjet|de|Tjet|ja|Tjet|zh|Tjet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tjet|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and ahead of him, four smaller figures serve as the king’s standard bearers at the head of the procession. These four standards represent the classic fourfold standard, which customarily symbolizes the divine attributes of the king. The {{ill|Falcon (disambiguation)|en|Falcon (disambiguation)|qid=Q223837|lt=Falcon|1=Falcon (disambiguation)}} portrays the king as the divine ruler, while the {{ill|Ibis (disambiguation)|en|Ibis (disambiguation)|qid=Q294079|lt=Ibis|1=Ibis (disambiguation)}} appears as the manifestation of the nocturnal {{ill|Moon Deity|de|Moon Deity|ja|Moon Deity|zh|Moon Deity|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moon Deity|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Thoth. Falcon and Ibis thus appeared as representatives of the two times of day – {{ill|Day (disambiguation)|en|Day (disambiguation)|qid=Q246899|lt=Day|1=Day (disambiguation)}} representing “activity”, “conquest”, “expansion” and the “sky god”, and {{ill|Night (disambiguation)|en|Night (disambiguation)|qid=Q69327093|lt=Night|1=Night (disambiguation)}} representing “procreation”, “magic”, the {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|qid=Q871389|lt=Land of Light of the Afterlife|1=Duat}} and the “{{ill|Centripetal Force|de|Centripetal Force|ja|Centripetal Force|zh|Centripetal Force|lt=Completer of the Cycle|lt_ja=Completer of the Cycle|lt_de=Completer of the Cycle|lt_zh=Completer of the Cycle|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Centripetal Force|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}”. The {{ill|Jackal (disambiguation)|en|Jackal (disambiguation)|qid=Q3157371|lt=Jackal|1=Jackal (disambiguation)}} or {{ill|Wolf|en|Wolf|qid=Q18498|lt=Wolf|1=Wolf}} served as the symbol for the deity Upuaut, who identified the king as the firstborn and, in a dual function, as the accompanying “Opener of the Ways” – both as the “Opener of the Ways” to the afterlife for the deceased and as the “Opener of the Ways” for the newborn in the birth canal. Finally, the {{ill|Chons Symbol|de|Chons Symbol|ja|Chons Symbol|zh|Chons Symbol|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chons Symbol|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} united the cycle of death and {{ill|Reincarnation|en|Reincarnation|qid=Q128593|lt=Reincarnation|1=Reincarnation}}. In {{ill|Egyptology|de|Egyptology|ja|Egyptology|zh|Egyptology|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Egyptology|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the fourfold standard is generally designated as the “Horus retinue”, although on other occasions the “open standard” also functioned as the “Horus retinue”. === Open Standard === The open standard appeared in iconographic representations whenever the king, as the “living Horus”, carried out the functions of “conqueror of enemies”, “lord of the world” and “conqueror”. The term “open standard” derives from the interchangeability of the depicted deities and was used in various ceremonies. {{ill|Seth (Egyptian mythology)|de|Seth (Egyptian mythology)|ja|Seth (Egyptian mythology)|zh|Seth (Egyptian mythology)|lt=Seth|lt_ja=Seth|lt_de=Seth|lt_zh=Seth|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seth (Egyptian mythology)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} could, prior to his interim {{ill|Vehmic court|en|Vehmic court|qid=Q639898|lt=defamation|1=Vehmic court}}, be taken into account in military conflicts just as {{ill|Min|en|Min|qid=Q224634|lt=Min|1=Min}} was in connection with the {{ill|King's Festival Calendar|de|Festkalender des Königs|ja|King's Festival Calendar|zh|King's Festival Calendar|lt=King's Renewal Festivals|lt_ja=King's Renewal Festivals|lt_de=King's Renewal Festivals|lt_zh=King's Renewal Festivals|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/King's Festival Calendar|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, for example, the {{ill|Min Festival|de|Min Festival|ja|Min Festival|zh|Min Festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Min Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Sed Festival|de|Sed Festival|ja|Sed Festival|zh|Sed Festival|lt=Sed Festival|lt_ja=Sed Festival|lt_de=Sed Festival|lt_zh=Sed Festival|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sed Festival|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Also belonging to the open standard and thus to the “Horus retinue” were female deities such as {{ill|Hathor|en|Hathor|qid=Q133343|lt=Hathor|1=Hathor}}, {{ill|Isis|en|Isis|qid=Q79876|lt=Isis|1=Isis}}, Mafdet and {{ill|Sekhmet|de|Sekhmet|ja|Sekhmet|zh|Sekhmet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekhmet|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In later Egyptian mythology, {{ill|Taweret|en|Taweret|qid=Q207435|lt=Taweret|1=Taweret}} is considered the “consort of Seth”, who was only admitted to the “Horus retinue” after her estrangement from Seth. == Festival Cult == === The Festival “Journey Through the Land” === {{Infobox hieroglyphs two-columns |TITEL = Königsbarke |NAME = D58-M17-G6-P3
Bak-wia
''Bjk-wj3''
''{{ill|Horus|en|Horus|qid=Q84122|lt=Falcon Barque (Royal Barque)|1=Horus}}'' |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Altes Reich (Ägypten)|de|Altes Reich (Ägypten)|ja|Altes Reich (Ägypten)|zh|Altes Reich (Ägypten)|lt=Old Kingdom (Egypt)|lt_ja=Old Kingdom (Egypt)|lt_de=Old Kingdom (Egypt)|lt_zh=Old Kingdom (Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Altes Reich (Ägypten)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} |BILD1 = Maquette-barque-khéops.jpg |BILD1-BREITE = 300px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Cheops’ barque as a Falcon Barque }} Originally, the king journeyed through the land every two years to administer justice and distribute gifts for outstanding achievements by the people. These activities were accompanied by the festival “Journey Through the Land”. In early times, village leaders and minor princes were responsible for the king’s provision. The standards marched alongside the king in the company of military detachments. In this context, the military unit was also regarded as the Horus retinue. The associated royal motif of “punishment of the wicked and reward for the loyal” persisted in various adaptations until the {{ill|Late Period (Egypt)|de|Late Period (Egypt)|ja|Late Period (Egypt)|zh|Late Period (Egypt)|lt=Late Period|lt_ja=Late Period|lt_de=Late Period|lt_zh=Late Period|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Period (Egypt)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In tomb paintings connected with the ceremonies, a ship was consistently depicted. This form of representation is attested in archaeological finds from the Negade-II period during the Naqada Culture (ca. 3500 to 3200 BC) throughout Egypt. Initially, the ship primarily served as a “container for gifts and offerings”. For this purpose, the royal ship – “Horus, the royal falcon” – symbolized the king himself as a divine emblem. The gift vessels likely contained ointments and {{ill|Anointing Oil|de|Anointing Oil|ja|Anointing Oil|zh|Anointing Oil|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Anointing Oil|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which the loyal of the land received as divine gifts. Even in later times, ointments and oils continued to hold great significance as sacred offerings. Sacrificial gifts from the populace could, in turn, be stored in the ship as it emptied. The king sailed on the ship—on which the Horus retinue was represented in the form of divine statues housed in small {{ill|Shrine|de|Shrine|ja|Shrine|zh|Shrine|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}s—making several stops along the banks of the {{ill|Nile (disambiguation)|en|Nile (disambiguation)|qid=Q45225|lt=Nile|1=Nile (disambiguation)}}.{{ill|Björn Landström|en|Björn Landström|qid=Q436856|lt=Björn Landström|1=Björn Landström}}: ''Ships of the Pharaohs. 4000 years of Egyptian shipbuilding.'' Allen & Unwin, London 1970, p. 120. While the people celebrated on the banks of the Nile, the king acted as the divine judge of his office. As a symbol of his jurisdiction, a boundary post was erected on the forward part of the ship with the installed execution device “Schemsi”, which the king replaced—after the introduction of the Re cult—with the “post of the world-ruler Re”. For example, the forty‐meter-long royal ship of {{ill|Khufu|en|Khufu|qid=Q161904|lt=Cheops|1=Khufu}} had a cladding of {{ill|Gold|en|Gold|qid=Q897|lt=Gold|1=Gold}} and served as the “{{ill|Judiciary|de|Judiciary|ja|Judiciary|zh|Judiciary|lt=great house of justice|lt_ja=great house of justice|lt_de=great house of justice|lt_zh=great house of justice|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Judiciary|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}”. === From the Horus Retinue to Tax Collection === Initially, only the counts of gold and {{ill|Agriculture|de|Agriculture|ja|Agriculture|zh|Agriculture|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Agriculture|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} produce were in focus; later, the cattle count was added. These activities, following the arrival of the {{ill|Nile Flood|de|Nile Flood|ja|Nile Flood|zh|Nile Flood|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nile Flood|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, were linked to the tasks of the royal judiciary. In this framework, the king’s officials delivered the tributes of the populace. The amount of the tribute was determined by the respective livestock and the arable land to be cultivated. The Horus retinue is attested on the {{ill|Palermo Stone|de|Palermo Stone|ja|Palermo Stone|zh|Palermo Stone|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Palermo Stone|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} since the {{ill|1st Dynasty|de|1st Dynasty|ja|1st Dynasty|zh|1st Dynasty|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/1st Dynasty|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In the second half of the 1st Dynasty, mentions of the Horus retinue initially ceased. Possibly, due to particular events, the Horus retinue temporarily receded into the background or was no longer explicitly mentioned. The exact reasons have not yet been reconstructed. However, with the beginning of the {{ill|2nd Dynasty|de|2nd Dynasty|ja|2nd Dynasty|zh|2nd Dynasty|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/2nd Dynasty|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, documented processions of the Horus retinue reappear, now supplemented with the addendum “x-th cattle count (Tenut)”. From this developed a tribute that was to be paid independently of the king’s presence. == The Horus Retinue in Funerary Cult == [[File:Horusgeleit1.PNG|thumb|Horus retinue as tomb painting]] After the king’s death, his body is escorted in a funeral procession to his tomb by the Horus retinue. The standard of Upuaut or {{ill|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|de|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|ja|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|zh|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|es|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|fr|Iounmoutef|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iunmutef|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was positioned at the head of the retinue. Upuaut is, like Anubis later, a {{ill|Totengott|de|Totengott|ja|Totengott|zh|Totengott|lt=Death God|lt_ja=Death God|lt_de=Death God|lt_zh=Death God|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Totengott|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who, as “Opener of the Ways”, accompanied the {{ill|Ba (Egyptian mythology)|de|Ba (Egyptian mythology)|ja|Ba (Egyptian mythology)|zh|Ba (Egyptian mythology)|lt=Ba souls|lt_ja=Ba souls|lt_de=Ba souls|lt_zh=Ba souls|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ba (Egyptian mythology)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on their journey to the {{ill|Ta-djeser|de|Ta-djeser|ja|Ta-djeser|zh|Ta-djeser|lt=holy land|lt_ja=holy land|lt_de=holy land|lt_zh=holy land|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-djeser|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Therefore, Upuaut and Anubis bore the epithet “Lord of the Holy Land”. In the later {{ill|Re (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (Egyptian mythology)|ja|Re (Egyptian mythology)|zh|Re (Egyptian mythology)|lt=Re cult|lt_ja=Re cult|lt_de=Re cult|lt_zh=Re cult|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Re (Egyptian mythology)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the reciting priests performed during the {{ill|Opening of the mouth ceremony|en|Opening of the mouth ceremony|qid=Q1191513|lt=Opening of the mouth ceremony|1=Opening of the mouth ceremony}} and in preparation for the “final procession of the justified” as the king of the living: “You set out and behold Re above the heavenly post, above the bearers of the sky, above the head of Iunmutef, above the shoulder of Upuaut.” In one tomb inscription, for example, the separation of the {{ill|Ka (Egyptian mythology)|de|Ka (Egyptian mythology)|ja|Ka (Egyptian mythology)|zh|Ka (Egyptian mythology)|lt=Ka|lt_ja=Ka|lt_de=Ka|lt_zh=Ka|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ka (Egyptian mythology)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} from the {{ill|Ba (Egyptian mythology)|de|Ba (Egyptian mythology)|ja|Ba (Egyptian mythology)|zh|Ba (Egyptian mythology)|lt=Ba|lt_ja=Ba|lt_de=Ba|lt_zh=Ba|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ba (Egyptian mythology)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is described: “May you free yourself from the Ka {{ill|sefech|de|Lösung (Altes Ägypten)|ja|Solution (Ancient Egypt)|zh|Solution (Ancient Egypt)|lt=free|lt_ja=free|lt_de=free|lt_zh=free|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/sefech|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, in the place where he (Re) dwells in the midst of the first Djadjat; may you become friendly with the Horus retinue.” In these ceremonies, Iunmutef, Anubis or Upuaut alternated as the “first of the Horus retinue”. At the same time, the “direction of the procession of the Horus retinue” makes it clear that the entrance gates of the {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|qid=Q871389|lt=afterlife|1=Duat}} were not located underground but in the celestial regions in the area of {{ill|Mesqet|de|Mesqet|ja|Mesqet|zh|Mesqet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mesqet|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. == Literature == * {{ill|Jan Assmann|en|Jan Assmann|qid=Q61247|lt=Jan Assmann|1=Jan Assmann}}: ''Death and the Afterlife in Ancient Egypt.'' Special edition. Beck, Munich 2003, ISBN 3-406-49707-1, p. 346. * Josep Cervelló Autuori: ''Ancient Africa. Ancient Egypt, an African Civilization'' ( = ''Aula Aegyptiaca.'' Studia 1). Proceedings of the IX Week of African Studies of the Centre d’Estudis Africans de Barcelona (18–22 March 1996). Aula Aegyptiaca, Barcelona 2001, ISBN 84-607-2429-8, pp. 77–96. * Hans Bonnet: ''Lexicon of Egyptian Religious History.'' Nikol, Hamburg 2005, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, p. 141. * {{ill|Wolfgang Helck|en|Wolfgang Helck|qid=Q74888|lt=Wolfgang Helck|1=Wolfgang Helck}}: ''Economic History of Ancient Egypt in the 3rd and 2nd Millennia BC.'' ( = ''{{ill|Handbuch der Orientalistik|de|Handbuch der Orientalistik|ja|Handbuch der Orientalistik|zh|Handbuch der Orientalistik|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Handbuch der Orientalistik|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. Section 1: The Near and Middle East. Volume 1: Egyptology. Chapter 5). Brill, Leiden et al. 1975, ISBN 90-04-04269-5. * Christoph Hönig: ''The Journey of Life on the Sea of the World. The Topos. Texts and Interpretations.'' Königshausen & Neumann, Würzburg 2000, ISBN 3-8260-1901-6, p. 20. * {{ill|Siegfried Schott|de|Siegfried Schott|ja|Siegfried Schott|zh|Siegfried Schott|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Siegfried Schott|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}: ''Ancient Egyptian Festival Dates'' ( = ''Academy of Sciences and Literature. Proceedings of the Humanities and Social Sciences Class (AM-GS)'' 1950, Volume 10, {{ISSN|0002-2977}}). Publisher of the Academy of Sciences and Literature, Mainz et al. 1950. == External Links == * [https://ancientegyptonline.co.uk/imiutf/ Hieroglyph R60: Imiut Fetish as Chons Symbol] == References == {{Egyptian Calendar Navbar Bar}} ==References== {{translated page|de|Horusgeleit|version=237237223|comment=Imported full de history}} [[de:Horusgeleit]] [[ja:ホルス随行]] [[de:Horus Retinue]] {{Egyptian Calendar Navbar}} [[Category:Pages with duplicated content]] ==Merged second translation== {{Infobox hieroglyphs two-columns |NAME = T18-S29-G43-D54:Z2-G5
Schemsu-{{ill|Horus|en|Horus|qid=Q84122|lt=Hor|1=Horus}}
''Šmsw-Ḥr.w''
''Horus Servants / Horus Retinue / Horus Following'' |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|qid=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom (Egypt)|1=Old Kingdom of Egypt}} |NAME2-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Military colours, standards and guidons|en|Military colours, standards and guidons|qid=Q2912707|lt=quadruple banner|1=Military colours, standards and guidons}} of the King The original hieroglyph of the banner of {{ill|Khonsu|en|Khonsu|qid=Q190521|lt=Khonsu|1=Khonsu}} (Imiut fetish) is currently not displayable in Wikipedia’s character set. |NAME2 = E18-G7-G26-R12-Aa1:N35:M23-G43
{{ill|Wepwawet|en|Wepwawet|qid=Q372560|lt=Upuaut|1=Wepwawet}}, {{ill|Horus|en|Horus|qid=Q84122|lt=Horus|1=Horus}}, {{ill|Thoth|en|Thoth|qid=Q146921|lt=Thoth|1=Thoth}} and {{ill|Khonsu|en|Khonsu|qid=Q190521|lt=Khonsu|1=Khonsu}} |NAME3-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Military colours, standards and guidons|en|Military colours, standards and guidons|qid=Q2912707|lt=open banner|1=Military colours, standards and guidons}} of the King |NAME3 = R12-E20-E15-G6-R23
{{ill|Seth (disambiguation)|en|Seth (disambiguation)|qid=Q971389|lt=Set|1=Seth (disambiguation)}}, {{ill|Anubis|en|Anubis|qid=Q47534|lt=Anubis|1=Anubis}}, {{ill|Horus|en|Horus|qid=Q84122|lt=Horus|1=Horus}} and {{ill|Min|en|Min|qid=Q224634|lt=Min|1=Min}} |BILD1 = NarmerPalette-CloseUpOfProcession-ROM.png |BILD1-BREITE = 300px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Horus Retinue on the Narmer Palette }} The '''Horus Retinue''' (also '''Servants of Horus, Festival of the Journey through the Land''') {{ill|Symbol|en|Symbol|qid=Q80071|lt=symbol|1=Symbol}} initially symbolized the {{ill|Pharaoh|en|Pharaoh|qid=Q37110|lt=King|1=Pharaoh}} as the living {{ill|Horus|en|Horus|qid=Q84122|lt=Horus|1=Horus}}, accompanied by other deities of {{ill|Ancient Egypt|en|Ancient Egypt|qid=Q11768|lt=ancient Egyptian|1=Ancient Egypt}}, and is attested in {{ill|Egyptian mythology|en|Egyptian mythology|qid=Q205740|lt=ancient Egyptian mythology|1=Egyptian mythology}} as early as the {{ill|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|en|Early Dynastic Period (Egypt)|qid=Q187979|lt=Thinite Period|1=Early Dynastic Period of Egypt}}. At that time, Horus was regarded as the all-encompassing sky god and is connected with the goddess {{ill|Mafdet|en|Mafdet|qid=Q845626|lt=Mafdet|1=Mafdet}}, who, as the bearer of the {{ill|Leopard skin (clothing in Ancient Egypt)|en|Leopard skin (clothing in Ancient Egypt)|qid=Q1486568|lt=sky panther fur|1=Leopard skin (clothing in Ancient Egypt)}}, possessed attributes later associated with {{ill|Osiris|en|Osiris|qid=Q46491|lt=Osiris|1=Osiris}}. With the introduction of the worship of the {{ill|Solar deity|en|Solar deity|qid=Q511056|lt=sun god|1=Solar deity}} {{ill|Ra|en|Ra|qid=Q1252904|lt=Ra|1=Ra}} in the {{ill|Third Dynasty of Egypt|en|Third Dynasty of Egypt|qid=Q220276|lt=Third Dynasty|1=Third Dynasty of Egypt}}, the meaning shifted, as the king then represented the personified sun deity as the firstborn son of Ra. With the later establishment of {{ill|Court|en|Court|qid=Q41487|lt=court|1=Court}} houses in {{ill|Memphis|en|Memphis|qid=Q4376240|lt=Memphis|1=Memphis}}, the processions of the Horus Retinue ended, along with the associated cattle census and judiciary. == Royal Cult == In the depictions of the {{ill|Turin King List|en|Turin King List|qid=Q622770|lt=Turin King List|1=Turin King List}}, the early kings are also listed and, with regard to the {{ill|Horus name|en|Horus name|qid=Q153263|lt=Horus name|1=Horus name}}, are explicitly designated as “Servants of Horus” or “Successors of Horus”. The calendrical entries on the {{ill|Palermo Stone|en|Palermo Stone|qid=Q308855|lt=Palermo Stone|1=Palermo Stone}} describe the cattle census, as part of tax contributions, as the solemn procession of the “Horus Retinue”. === Quadruple Banner === On the {{ill|Narmer Palette|en|Narmer Palette|qid=Q368138|lt=Narmer Palette|1=Narmer Palette}}, during a procession, the king wears the {{ill|Lower Egypt|en|Lower Egypt|qid=Q463871|lt=red crown of Lower Egypt|1=Lower Egypt}}, followed by a sandal-bearer whose title is marked with the {{ill|Ideogram|en|Ideogram|qid=Q138619|lt=ideogram|1=Ideogram}} of the goddess {{ill|Seshat|en|Seshat|qid=Q478490|lt=Seschat|1=Seshat}}. Preceding the king is a bearer of panther fur with the designation {{ill|Tjet|de|Tjet|ja|トジェット|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tjet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tjet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tjet|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and ahead of him, four smaller figures serve as the standard-bearers at the head of the procession. These four standards represent the classic quadruple banner, which usually symbolizes the divine attributes of the king. The {{ill|Falcon|en|Falcon|qid=Q43489|lt=falcon|1=Falcon}} portrays the king as the divine ruler, while the {{ill|Ibis|en|Ibis|qid=Q193833|lt=ibis|1=Ibis}} appears as the manifestation of the nocturnal {{ill|Lunar deity|en|Lunar deity|qid=Q1569856|lt=moon deity|1=Lunar deity}}, Thoth. The falcon and the ibis thus act as representatives of the two parts of the day, {{ill|Day|en|Day|qid=Q573|lt=day|1=Day}}, symbolizing “activity”, “conquest”, and “expansion” as well as “sky god”, and {{ill|Night|en|Night|qid=Q575|lt=night|1=Night}}, symbolizing “procreation”, “magic”, the “Land of Light of the Afterlife” ({{ill|Duat|en|Duat|qid=Q871389|lt=Land of Light of the Afterlife|1=Duat}}) and the “executor of the cycle” ({{ill|Centripetal force|en|Centripetal force|qid=Q172881|lt=executor of the cycle|1=Centripetal force}}). The {{ill|Jackal|en|Jackal|qid=Q125525|lt=jackal|1=Jackal}} or {{ill|Wolf|en|Wolf|qid=Q18498|lt=wolf|1=Wolf}} served as a sign for the deity {{ill|Wepwawet|en|Wepwawet|qid=Q372560|lt=Upuaut|1=Wepwawet}}, who, in his dual role as the firstborn and “path opener”, signified the king—both as the opener of the way to the afterlife for the deceased and as the opener of the path for the newborn in the birth canal. Finally, the {{ill|Khonsu symbol|de|Chons-Symbol|ja|コンスの象徴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Khonsu symbol|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Khonsu symbol|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Khonsu symbol|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} united the cycle of death and {{ill|Reincarnation|en|Reincarnation|qid=Q128593|lt=reincarnation|1=Reincarnation}}. In Egyptology, the quadruple banner is most often referred to as the “Horus Retinue”, although on other occasions the “open banner” also functioned as the “Horus Retinue”. === Open Banner === The open banner appears in iconographic representations whenever the king, as the “living Horus”, carries out his functions as “subduer of enemies”, “lord of the world” and “conqueror”. The term “open banner” derives from the interchangeability of the depicted deities and was used in various ceremonies. {{ill|Seth (disambiguation)|en|Seth (disambiguation)|qid=Q971389|lt=Set|1=Seth (disambiguation)}} could, before his interim {{ill|Vehmic court|en|Vehmic court|qid=Q639898|lt=vilification|1=Vehmic court}}, be considered in martial conflicts just as {{ill|Min|en|Min|qid=Q224634|lt=Min|1=Min}} was in connection with the king's renewal festivals—such as the {{ill|Min festival|en|Min festival|qid=Q1936130|lt=Min festival|1=Min festival}} and the {{ill|Sed festival|en|Sed festival|qid=Q604644|lt=Sed festival|1=Sed festival}}. The open banner, and thus the “Horus Retinue”, also included goddesses such as {{ill|Hathor|en|Hathor|qid=Q133343|lt=Hathor|1=Hathor}}, {{ill|Isis|en|Isis|qid=Q79876|lt=Isis|1=Isis}}, Mafdet and {{ill|Sekhmet|en|Sekhmet|qid=Q146104|lt=Sekhmet|1=Sekhmet}}. In later Egyptian mythology, {{ill|Taweret|en|Taweret|qid=Q207435|lt=Taweret|1=Taweret}} is regarded as the “consort of Seth”, having been included in the “Horus Retinue” only after her separation from Seth. == Festival Cult == === The Festival "Journey through the Land" === {{Infobox hieroglyphs two-columns |TITEL = Royal Barque |NAME = D58-M17-G6-P3
Bak-wia
''Bjk-wj3''
''{{ill|Horus|en|Horus|qid=Q84122|lt=falcon barque (royal barque)|1=Horus}}'' |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Old Kingdom of Egypt|en|Old Kingdom of Egypt|qid=Q177819|lt=Old Kingdom (Egypt)|1=Old Kingdom of Egypt}} |BILD1 = Maquette-barque-khéops.jpg |BILD1-BREITE = 300px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Cheops’ barque as a falcon barque }} Originally, the king journeyed through the land every two years to administer justice and distribute gifts for outstanding achievements by the populace. These activities were accompanied by the festival “Journey through the Land”. In early times, village chiefs and minor princes were responsible for supplying the king. The banners, along with military detachments, accompanied the king on his journey. In this context, the army also served as the Horus Retinue. The related royal motif of “punishment of the wicked and reward of the loyal” persisted in various forms until the {{ill|Late Period (Egypt)|de|Spätzeit (Ägypten)|ja|後期エジプト|lt=Late Period|lt_ja=後期|lt_de=Spätzeit|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Period (Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Period (Egypt)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Late Period (Egypt)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. In tomb paintings, a ship was consistently depicted in connection with the ceremonies. This mode of depiction is attested in archaeological finds throughout Egypt from the {{ill|Naqada culture|en|Naqada culture|qid=Q753391|lt=Naqada II period (ca. 3500 to 3200 BC)|1=Naqada culture}}. Initially, the ship primarily served as a “container for gifts and offerings”. For this purpose, the royal ship represented the king himself as the divine symbol “Horus, the Royal Falcon”. The gift vessels likely contained ointments and {{ill|anointing oils|de|Salböle|ja|聖油|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/anointing oils|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/anointing oils|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/anointing oils|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, which the loyal subjects received as sacred gifts. Even later, ointments and oils continued to hold great significance as holy offerings. The offerings of the people could, in turn, be stored in the ship as it emptied. The king sailed on the ship—on which the Horus Retinue appeared in the form of statues of deities housed in small {{ill|shrines|de|Schreine|ja|聖廟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 廟|1=Miao shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q29043583}}—making several stops along the bank of the {{ill|Nile|en|Nile|qid=Q3392|lt=Nile|1=Nile}}.{{ill|Björn Landström|en|Björn Landström|qid=Q436856|lt=Björn Landström|1=Björn Landström}}: ''Ships of the Pharaohs. 4000 Years of Egyptian Shipbuilding.'' Allen & Unwin, London 1970, p. 120. While the people celebrated on the riverbank, the king executed his office as a divine judge. As a symbol of his judicial authority, a boundary post with the execution device “Schemsi” was erected at the front of the ship—a device later replaced after the introduction of the Ra Cult by the “post of the world-ruler Ra”. For example, the 40-meter-long royal ship of {{ill|Khufu|en|Khufu|qid=Q161904|lt=Cheops|1=Khufu}} was clad in {{ill|Gold|en|Gold|qid=Q897|lt=Metal (金)|1=Gold}} and served as a “great house of justice”. === From the Horus Retinue to Tax Collection === At first, only the censuses of gold and {{ill|Agriculture|en|Agriculture|qid=Q11451|lt=agricultural yield|1=Agriculture}} production were central; later, the cattle census was added. These activities, following the arrival of the {{ill|Flooding of the Nile|en|Flooding of the Nile|qid=Q1519552|lt=annual Nile flood|1=Flooding of the Nile}}, were linked to the tasks of royal justice. In this context, the king’s officials collected the people’s taxes, the amount of which was determined by the number of cattle and the extent of arable land. The Horus Retinue is attested on the Palermo Stone from the {{ill|First Dynasty of Egypt|en|First Dynasty of Egypt|qid=Q203859|lt=First Dynasty|1=First Dynasty of Egypt}}. In the second half of the First Dynasty, mentions of the Horus Retinue initially ceased. Possibly, due to specific events, the Horus Retinue temporarily receded into the background or was no longer explicitly mentioned. The exact reasons have not yet been reconstructed. With the beginning of the {{ill|Second Dynasty of Egypt|en|Second Dynasty of Egypt|qid=Q207778|lt=Second Dynasty|1=Second Dynasty of Egypt}}, however, attested processions of the Horus Retinue reappear—now supplemented with the annotation “x-th census (Tenut)”—from which developed a tax that was to be paid regardless of the king’s appearance. == The Horus Retinue in Funerary Cult == [[File:Horusgeleit1.PNG|thumb|Horus Retinue as Tomb Painting]] After the king’s death, his body is escorted by the Horus Retinue in a funeral procession to his tomb. The banner of {{ill|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|de|Iunmutef|1=Iunmutef|2=de|3=Iunmutef|4=es|5=Iunmutef|6=fr|7=Iounmoutef|8=fi|9=Inmutef|ja|イウンムテフ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iunmutef|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iunmutef|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iunmutef|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was positioned at the head of the procession. Upuaut, like later {{ill|Anubis|en|Anubis|qid=Q47534|lt=Anubis|1=Anubis}}, is a {{ill|god of the dead|de|Totengott|ja|死の神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/god of the dead|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/god of the dead|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/god of the dead|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who, as the “opener of the ways”, accompanied the {{ill|Ba|de|Ba (Egyptian Mythology)|ja|バー|lt=Ba souls|lt_ja=バーの魂|lt_de=Ba-Seelen|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ba|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ba|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ba|1=Ba|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11328469}} on their journey to the “holy land” ({{ill|Ta-djeser|de|Ta-djeser|ja|タ・ジェセル|lt=holy land|lt_ja=聖地|lt_de=heilige Land|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-djeser|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-djeser|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ta-djeser|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}). Therefore, Upuaut and Anubis bore the epithet “Lord of the Holy Land”. In the later {{ill|Ra|en|Ra|qid=Q1252904|lt=Ra Cult|1=Ra}}, the reciting priests chanted during the {{ill|Opening of the mouth ceremony|en|Opening of the mouth ceremony|qid=Q1191513|lt=Opening of the mouth ceremony|1=Opening of the mouth ceremony}} and in preparation for the “final march of the justified” as the king of the living: “You set out and behold Ra above the heavenly post, the bearers of the heavens, above the head of Iunmutef, above the shoulder of Upuaut.” In one tomb text, for instance, the separation of the {{ill|Ka (Egyptian soul)|en|Ka (Egyptian soul)|qid=Q360536|lt=Ka|1=Ka (Egyptian soul)}} from the {{ill|Ba_(Egyptian_soul)|en|Ba_(Egyptian_soul)|qid=Q1129404|lt=Ba|1=Ba (Egyptian soul)}} is described: “May you detach yourself from the Ka at the place where he (Ra) dwells among the first Djadjat; may you befriend the Horus Retinue.” Iunmutef, Anubis, or Upuaut alternated in these ceremonies as the “leader of the Horus Retinue”. At the same time, the “direction of the movement of the Horus Retinue” makes it clear that the entrance gates to the {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|qid=Q871389|lt=afterlife|1=Duat}} were not located underground but in the celestial regions in the area of {{ill|Mesqet|de|Mesqet|ja|メスクェト|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mesqet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mesqet|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mesqet|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. == Literature == * {{ill|Jan Assmann|en|Jan Assmann|qid=Q61247|lt=Jan Assmann|1=Jan Assmann}}: ''Death and the Afterlife in Ancient Egypt.'' Special Edition. Beck, Munich 2003, ISBN 3-406-49707-1, p. 346. * Josep Cervelló Autuori: ''Ancient Africa. Ancient Egypt, an African Civilization'' ( = ''Aula Aegyptiaca.'' Studia 1). Proceedings of the IX Week of African Studies of the Centre d’Estudis Africans de Barcelona (18–22 March 1996). Aula Aegyptiaca, Barcelona 2001, ISBN 84-607-2429-8, pp. 77–96. * Hans Bonnet: ''Lexicon of Egyptian Religious History.'' Nikol, Hamburg 2005, ISBN 3-937872-08-6, p. 141. * {{ill|Wolfgang Helck|en|Wolfgang Helck|qid=Q74888|lt=Wolfgang Helck|1=Wolfgang Helck}}: ''Economic History of Ancient Egypt in the 3rd and 2nd Millennia BC.'' ( = ''Handbook of Oriental Studies.'' Section 1: ''The Near and Middle East.'' Volume 1: ''Egyptology.'' Part 5). Brill, Leiden et al. 1975, ISBN 90-04-04269-5. * Christoph Hönig: ''The Voyage of Life on the Sea of the World. The Topos. Texts and Interpretations.'' Königshausen & Neumann, Würzburg 2000, ISBN 3-8260-1901-6, p. 20. * {{ill|Siegfried Schott|de|Siegfried Schott|ja|ジークフリート・ショット|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Siegfried Schott|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Siegfried Schott|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Siegfried Schott|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135491464|1=Siegfried Schott}}: ''Ancient Egyptian Festive Dates'' ( = ''Academy of Sciences and Literature. Proceedings of the Humanities and Social Sciences Class. (AM-GS).'' 1950, Volume 10, {{ISSN|0002-2977}}). Published by the Academy of Sciences and Literature, Mainz et al., 1950. == External Links == * [https://ancientegyptonline.co.uk/imiutf/] == References == {{Egyptian Calendar Navigation}} {{translated page|de|Horusgeleit|version=237237223|comment=Imported full de history}} [[de:Horusgeleit]] [[ja:ホルス従者]] [[de:Horus Retinue]] {{wikidata link|Q1630274}} [[Category:Ancient Egyptian festivals]] [[Category:Festivals of ancient Egypt]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:WikiProject Ancient Egypt]]
